RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 11: Why Does 1 == '1' Return true?
Day 11 of the reincarnation. This time we're going to look at type coercion in JavaScript — one of those concepts that's super common and genuinely important to get right.
Before we get into type coercion, we need to figure out what kind of language JavaScript actually is. You've probably heard people say JavaScript is a weakly typed language. So what does that mean?
Weak typing usually gets compared against strong typing. The main thing about a weakly typed language is that it
converts types for you automatically (this is also called implicit conversion)

. To be fair, the strong/weak labels aren't carved in stone — you've probably also heard terms like static typing and dynamic typing. If we're talking specifically about JavaScript, then JavaScript is a

Okay, honestly I'm going to stop there, because I'm not totally sure how to explain it simply and I don't want to mislead anyone. For a proper intro to types, I'd point you at this article from someone who knows it way better than me →
你不可不知的 JavaScript 二三事#Day3：資料型態的夢魘——動態型別加弱型別(2) (article in Mandarin)

But if I really have to give an analogy: weak typing is like getting reincarnated into another world where the god of that world loves you so much they hand you every skill ready to go, and even auto-cast them for you (automatic type conversion). The catch is, the moment your skills get sealed (a type error), you're basically dead and there's nothing you can do. And strong typing? That's getting reincarnated with zero skills — you have to grind and earn everything yourself. So when your skills get sealed, it's no problem at all because you built everything from scratch.
I know that analogy is pretty rough, but it's just one way to make the idea easier to hold onto.

Generally speaking, if you don't sort out how type coercion works, it's going to turn into a nightmare for you as a JavaScript developer. Here's an example. We already know by now that any value wrapped in single or double quotes is a string. So why is it that when we use the double-equals operator, we get

The reason comes down to JavaScript itself. JavaScript is nosy, takes liberties, and thinks it's being clever (it isn't) by doing type conversion for you — it quietly converts the values to the same type and
compares them. Here's a diagram using the example above ↓
And because of this behavior, you get all kinds of weird bugs. To deal with it, there's a strict comparison operator — three equals signs,
. You can think of three equals as you telling JavaScript: "Hey! JavaScript, don't go quietly converting types on me — I want to compare the current types exactly as they are!"
This is exactly why you'll find a bunch of classic head-scratcher examples online:
And once you switch to strict comparison, you'll find they all turn into
For the kind of dev work we're doing now, I'd personally recommend using strict comparison rather than loose comparison (
), because the automatic type conversion in loose comparison makes it really easy to run into strange problems.

Of course you might say "then I'll just write a strongly typed language instead." The thing is, no language is absolutely good or bad. Strongly typed languages are stricter, sure, and most problems show up while you're writing the code. But the upside of a weakly typed language is that it's less fussy to write and gives you more flexibility (huh?!
I can't think of any other upsides right now

In the end, if you want to avoid these automatic-conversion incidents, my personal suggestion is to turn on an ESLint style manager, or learn something like TypeScript — those are both valid ways to deal with it.
Since I'm personally not that familiar with TypeScript (I've only written basic TypeScript), here's the TypeScript Wikipedia page for reference:

Day 11 of the reincarnation. This time we dig into type coercion in JavaScript — one of the most common and most important concepts in the language. We'll talk about weak vs strong typing, why JavaScript loves to convert types behind your back, and why == can return some genuinely weird results.
